turns.user_message: I've tried to quit 3 times. The longest I went was 3 months. But I just can't seem to stay quit. My daughter is growing up and I don't want her to see me like this.
step2_rag_chunks: If you have tried to quit before, that experience is actually useful information â€” not a failure. What worked even a little? What made it harder? Most people who successfully quit have tried several times before it sticks. Each attempt teaches you something about your own pattern.
Relapse can happen during the quitting process. Instead of feeling discouraged, treat it as a learning experience and continue trying to quit.

It is completely normal to want to quit and also feel unsure about it at the same time. Most people feel this way â€” one part of them wants to stop, another part is not ready. That conflict is not weakness, it is just how change works. It helps to talk about both sides openly rather than pushing the doubts aside.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the EXPLORATION phase. You know the basics. Now gently explore what situations or emotions make them smoke more — triggers like stress, meals, social settings, boredom. Do NOT suggest any strategies yet. Just understand their situation.

Therapist: It sounds like you're feeling a little more hopeful about trying a discreet fidget toy to help manage your cravings. What specific situations or triggers do you think you might use the fidget toy to help calm yourself in, like during morning cravings or work stress?

Patient: I think the driving thing would be tough, but during meetings and when I'm getting anxious about work, it might actually help. And mornings, yeah, mornings are usually when I crave a cigarette the most, so maybe that's something I could try using the fidget toy for.

step2_rag_chunks: After meals, immediately introduce a competing behavior before the urge has a chance to form â€” get up from the table, brush your teeth, chew sugar-free gum, or take a short walk. The goal is to break the automatic link between finishing a meal and reaching for a cigarette by making a different behavior equally automatic over time.
Keeping your hands busy with activities such as drawing, writing, or using a stress ball can help reduce the urge to smoke.

When a craving hits, delaying the response by even 5 to 10 minutes is often enough for it to pass. Tell yourself you will smoke in 10 minutes if you still want to â€” and then do something else for those 10 minutes. Most of the time the urge has weakened significantly by the time the 10 minutes are up.

One sentence only. Nothing more.

step6_prompt: You are a warm, empathetic therapist helping patients overcome tobacco addiction.
STYLE RULES — follow these strictly:
- Keep your response to 1-3 sentences maximum
- Ask only ONE question per response
- Do NOT list techniques, bullet points, or dump information
- First acknowledge what the patient said, then ask ONE follow-up question
- Only suggest a technique when you are in the Planning phase
- Use simple, conversational language — not clinical or formal
